beginner.question: What is a CSS selector?
beginner.answer: A CSS selector is a pattern used to select and target HTML elements for styling. It tells the browser which elements a set of CSS rules should apply to. For example, h1 selects all <h1> elements, .class selects all elements with that class, and #id selects the element with that specific ID.

beginner.question: What is the difference between a class selector and an ID selector?
beginner.answer: A class selector (.classname) can be reused on multiple elements and an element can have multiple classes. An ID selector (#idname) must be unique on the page — only one element can have a given ID. Classes are for reusable styling; IDs are for unique page elements or JavaScript hooks.

beginner.question: What does the universal selector (*) do?
beginner.answer: The universal selector (*) selects all elements on the page. It's commonly used in CSS resets to apply default styles like margin: 0 and padding: 0 to every element. However, it should be used sparingly due to performance implications — it forces the browser to check every element.

beginner.question: How do you select multiple elements that should share the same styles?
beginner.answer: You group selectors with commas. For example: h1, h2, h3 { font-family: Arial; } applies the same styles to all h1, h2, and h3 elements. This reduces code duplication and makes stylesheets easier to maintain.

beginner.question: What is a type (element) selector in CSS?
beginner.answer:
A type or element selector targets all elements of a specific HTML tag name. For example, p { color: blue; } selects every

element on the page. Similarly, div { margin: 10px; } selects all

elements. Type selectors have a specificity of 0-0-0-1.

beginner.question: What is the descendant combinator and how is it written?
beginner.answer:
The descendant combinator is a space between two selectors. It selects all elements matching the second selector that are descendants (at any level) of an element matching the first selector. Example: div p { color: red; } selects all

elements that are inside any

, regardless of how deeply nested.

beginner.question: What is the difference between a class and an ID in terms of specificity?
beginner.answer: A class selector has a specificity of 0-0-1-0, while an ID selector has a specificity of 0-1-0-0. This means an ID selector is 10 times more specific than a class selector in the specificity calculation. A single ID will override any number of class selectors.

beginner.question: How do pseudo-classes differ from pseudo-elements?
beginner.answer: Pseudo-classes (single colon :) target elements based on their state or position, such as :hover, :focus, :first-child, or :nth-child(). Pseudo-elements (double colon ::) target specific parts of elements or generate content, such as ::before, ::after, ::first-line, and ::selection.

beginner.question: What does the :hover pseudo-class do?
beginner.answer: The :hover pseudo-class applies styles when the user's mouse pointer is over an element. It's commonly used for interactive elements like links and buttons to provide visual feedback, such as changing color, adding underlines, or applying transform effects on mouseover.

beginner.question: How do you select a child element that is a direct child of a parent?
beginner.answer: Use the child combinator (>). Example: ul > li { list-style: none; } selects only <li> elements that are direct children of a <ul>, but not nested <li> elements inside sub-lists. This is more specific than the descendant combinator (space).

intermediate.question: What is the specificity calculation for the selector `div.container#main p.active`?
intermediate.answer: Breaking it down: div (element = 0-0-0-1), .container (class = 0-0-1-0), #main (ID = 0-1-0-0), p (element = 0-0-0-1), .active (class = 0-0-1-0). Total specificity: 0-1-2-2 (one ID, two classes, two elements).

intermediate.question: What is the :nth-child() pseudo-class and how does the An+B syntax work?
intermediate.answer: :nth-child(An+B) selects elements based on their position among siblings. 'A' is the step size, 'n' starts at 0, and 'B' is the offset. :nth-child(2n) selects even elements (2nd, 4th, 6th...). :nth-child(2n+1) selects odd ones. :nth-child(3n) selects every 3rd element. Keywords 'even' and 'odd' are shortcuts for 2n and 2n+1.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between :first-child and :first-of-type?
intermediate.answer:
:first-child selects an element only if it is the first child of its parent, regardless of element type. :first-of-type selects the first element of its type among siblings, even if other element types appear before it. For example, in
1

2

, p:first-child does NOT match but p:first-of-type DOES match.

intermediate.question: What are attribute selectors and what operators are available for pattern matching?
intermediate.answer: Attribute selectors target elements based on HTML attributes. Operators include: [attr] (has attribute), [attr="value"] (exact match), [attr^="value"] (starts with), [attr$="value"] (ends with), [attr*="value"] (contains substring), [attr~="value"] (word in space-separated list), and [attr|="value"] (starts with value followed by hyphen).

intermediate.question: Why does a space between a selector and :hover change the meaning?
intermediate.answer: A space is the descendant combinator. element :hover (with space) selects any child element that is in hover state. element:hover (no space) selects the element itself when hovered. For example, div :hover selects any hovered descendant of div, while div:hover selects the div itself when hovered.

intermediate.question: What is the :not() pseudo-class and what can be passed as an argument?
intermediate.answer: :not() is the negation pseudo-class — it selects elements that do NOT match the selector passed as argument. Example: input:not([type="submit"]) selects all inputs except submit buttons. In CSS3, :not() only accepts simple selectors (type, class, ID, attribute, pseudo-class). CSS4 (Level 4) allows full selector lists inside :not().

intermediate.question: How do browsers read selectors (left-to-right or right-to-left) and why does it matter?
intermediate.answer: Modern browsers read selectors right-to-left (bottom-up). For div.container ul li a, the browser first finds all <a> elements, then checks if they have an <li> ancestor, then a <ul> ancestor, then a div.container ancestor. This is more efficient because it starts with the smallest set of elements. This is why the rightmost part of a selector (the key selector) matters most for performance.

intermediate.question: What is the ::selection pseudo-element and how is it styled?
intermediate.answer: ::selection targets the portion of text that a user has highlighted/selected. It accepts a limited set of properties: color, background-color, cursor, caret-color, outline, text-decoration, text-shadow, and background. Example: ::selection { background: yellow; color: black; } creates a custom highlight color.

intermediate.question: How do you use the adjacent sibling combinator (+)?
intermediate.answer:
The adjacent sibling combinator (+) selects an element that is immediately preceded by a specific sibling. Example: h2 + p selects the first

that directly follows an

. If there's another element between them, the selector doesn't match. This is useful for styling the first paragraph after a heading without adding a class.

intermediate.question: What is the general sibling combinator (~) and how does it differ from +?
intermediate.answer:
The general sibling combinator (~) selects all siblings that follow the specified element, not just the immediately adjacent one. Example: h2 ~ p selects all

elements that are siblings and appear after any

. The adjacent sibling (+) selects only the first immediately following sibling, while general sibling (~) selects all following siblings.

advanced.question: Explain how specificity is computed for the :is(), :where(), :not(), and :has() pseudo-class functions.
advanced.answer: :is() and :not() take the specificity of their most specific argument. For example, :is(#id, .class) has 0-1-0-0 specificity (from #id). :where() always has 0-0-0-0 specificity regardless of its arguments — it's designed to add no specificity. :has() follows the same rule as :is() — it takes the specificity of its most specific argument.

advanced.question: What is the :has() pseudo-class and what makes it revolutionary for CSS?
advanced.answer:
:has() is a relational pseudo-class that selects an element based on its descendants or siblings. It's often called the 'parent selector' because it allows CSS to select a parent element based on its children. Example: div:has(> p) selects div elements that have a direct child

. It also enables previous-sibling selection: h1:has(+ p) selects an h1 that's directly followed by a p. :has() is supported in all modern browsers as of 2024.

advanced.question: How do you debug specificity conflicts in complex selectors with multiple pseudo-classes and combinators?
advanced.answer: Use browser DevTools to inspect the element and view the 'Computed' styles panel. It shows which rules are applied (and which are overridden/struck through) with their source locations. Calculate specificity manually by counting IDs (0-1-0-0 each), classes/attributes/pseudo-classes (0-0-1-0 each), and elements/pseudo-elements (0-0-0-1 each). The specificity calculator in DevTools also shows the numeric value. Common issues arise from :is() and :not() which take the highest specificity of any argument.

advanced.question: How do CSS nesting (in CSS Native) affect selector specificity?
advanced.answer: CSS Nesting (the & nesting feature, now standard) does not change specificity rules. The & refers to the parent selector, and specificity is calculated from the fully expanded selector. For example, .card { & .title { color: red; } } is equivalent to .card .title { color: red; } with specificity 0-0-2-0 (two classes). Deeper nesting still follows standard specificity — it doesn't magically increase specificity beyond the actual selector components.

advanced.question: Explain how the :focus-visible pseudo-class works and why it matters for accessibility.
advanced.answer: :focus-visible applies styles only when the browser determines the focus should be visible, typically when the user navigates via keyboard (Tab key) rather than mouse click. This solves the problem of removing focus outlines for mouse users (who don't need them) while preserving them for keyboard users (who rely on them). The modern pattern is: :focus:not(:focus-visible) { outline: none; } to remove mouse focus rings while keeping keyboard indicators.

advanced.question: What is the difference between :nth-child(An+B) and :nth-of-type(An+B) when mixed element types are present?
advanced.answer:
:nth-child counts all siblings regardless of element type. :nth-of-type counts only siblings matching the element type. Example:

A

B

C

— p:nth-child(2) selects nothing (the second child is ), but p:nth-of-type(2) selects

C

(the second

element). Understanding this distinction is crucial when elements of different types are interleaved.

advanced.question: How do you select elements based on their text direction using attribute selectors?
advanced.answer: You can use the [dir] attribute selector to target elements by text direction. [dir="ltr"] selects left-to-right elements, [dir="rtl"] selects right-to-left elements. You can also use the |=-prefix operator: [lang|="en"] selects elements with lang="en" or lang="en-US". This is useful for internationalization where different scripts may need different font families or alignment.

advanced.question: What is the :target pseudo-class and what practical use does it have?
advanced.answer: :target selects the element whose ID matches the current URL fragment (hash). For example, if the URL ends with #section2, then #section2:target { background: yellow; } highlights that section. Practical uses include: highlighting the linked section in long documents, creating tab interfaces, implementing accordions without JavaScript (using :target with :not(:target) to hide/show content), and single-page navigation highlighting.

advanced.question: How do you write a selector that targets an element with multiple classes, and how does the order of classes in the selector matter?
advanced.answer: To target elements with multiple specific classes, chain them without spaces: .class1.class2 selects elements that have BOTH class1 AND class2. The order of classes in the selector doesn't matter — .class1.class2 matches the same elements as .class2.class1. However, selector order affects specificity calculation (each class adds 0-0-1-0). The classes in the HTML attribute order also don't matter.

advanced.question: Explain how the :empty pseudo-class works and what elements it considers empty.
advanced.answer: :empty selects elements that have NO children at all, including text nodes (even whitespace). An element with a space or newline is NOT considered empty by the original :empty definition. However, CSS Level 4 introduced :empty to also ignore whitespace-only text nodes. Practical use: hiding empty alert boxes (div.alert:empty { display: none; }) or adding placeholder content to structurally empty elements.
